summary: 経済アナリスト・森永卓郎が自身の集めた膨大な「B級コレクション」を展示する個人運営の私設博物館。公式タグラインは「ビンボーでおバカだけど、ビューティフルな博物館」。一見ガラクタにしか見えない大量生産時代の日用品・パッケージ・玩具・グリコのおまけ・牛乳キャップ・ペットボトルキャップ・ミニカー・キン肉マン消しゴム・空き缶ラベル等を、徹底的に分類・体系化することで、近代日本の消費史・広告史・流通史を映す独自の「歴史/アート」へ反転させる。2014年（平成26年）開館、住宅地に建つコンクリート三階建ての小さな建物に数十万点規模のコレクションが密集する。通常の公的博物館に対する「私設・B級・自己定義」の極北として、B級スポット文化の中核に位置づけられる。
highlights: 公式タグライン「ビンボーでおバカだけど、ビューティフルな博物館」
大量生産時代の日用品・パッケージ・玩具・おまけを徹底分類した一点突破型コレクション
森永卓郎が個人運営する稀有な経済アナリスト主宰の私設博物館
from_kameyama: 車で約420分（東名阪→新東名→首都圏中央連絡道経由）／新幹線＋私鉄で約4時間（亀山→名古屋→東京→所沢→新所沢／航空公園）
summary_en: B-Hōkan — roughly, 'Museum of B-Grade Treasures' — is the private museum of economist and television commentator Morinaga Takurō, opened in 2014 in a small three-story concrete building in suburban Tokorozawa. The official tagline is 'a poor, foolish, but beautiful museum,' and the contents make good on the promise: hundreds of thousands of items of mass-produced postwar Japanese consumer flotsam, painstakingly categorized and displayed as the social history of a particular country between roughly 1950 and the present. Glico caramel-box prizes are arranged by year. Plastic milk-bottle caps are sorted by region. Kinnikuman gum erasers, empty cigarette packs, vintage matchbox labels, and 1970s soft-drink cans are filed by manufacturer. The argument is the same throughout: that which the consumer society throws away is the actual record of how the consumer society lived, and one obsessive economist with a working knowledge of taxonomy can flip 'trash' into 'archive' simply by classifying it correctly. Open by appointment only; small, residential, and one of the strangest serious arguments for what a museum can be in the country.
highlights_en: Hundreds of thousands of pieces of mass-produced postwar consumer flotsam — Glico prizes, milk-bottle caps, gum erasers, matchbox labels — meticulously classified by year, region and manufacturer
Personally collected and curated by economist and television commentator Morinaga Takurō; the museum is his thesis about how to read a consumer society
Appointment-only access in a small residential building — one of the strangest serious arguments for what a museum can be in Japan

summary: 福井県勝山市の山あいに突如そびえる、座高約17mの大毘盧遮那仏を本尊とする巨大寺院。地元タクシー会社・相互タクシーの創業者が1987年（昭和62年）に私財を投じて建立した、稀有な「個人発願の巨大伽藍」として知られる。境内には大仏殿のほか高さ75mの五重塔、回廊、九龍壁、池泉回遊式庭園などが配され、大仏殿内部の壁面には数千体規模の小仏（公式・地域案内によれば計1,281体とも伝わる石仏・諸仏が境内・回廊・大仏殿に分布）が整然と並ぶ。建立当時は奈良の大仏を上回る大きさとして話題を集め、現在も観光・宗教施設として営業を継続。中部・北陸の巨像系B級スポットの代表格で、近隣の白山平泉寺・恐竜博物館と合わせた周遊先となっている。
highlights: 座高約17m 個人発願（相互タクシー創業者）が建立した巨大大毘盧遮那仏

境内・大仏殿・回廊に分布する1,281体伝の石仏・諸仏群

summary_en: In the mountains of Katsuyama, far enough from anywhere else that the first glimpse of it from the access road is genuinely startling, a 17-meter seated Buddha sits inside a colossal modern temple hall, surrounded by a 75-meter five-story pagoda, a long covered cloister, a stroll garden with ponds, and approximately 1,281 smaller Buddhas distributed across the precinct. The entire complex was built between 1987 and the early 1990s by a single individual — Tada Kiyoshi, the founder of a local taxi company — who funded the project from his own fortune with the explicit ambition of building a temple larger than Nara. He succeeded: the seated Buddha is several meters taller than the great Buddha of Tōdai-ji, and the surrounding pagoda is one of the tallest in Japan. The complex never achieved the visitor numbers it was built for, and there is now something melancholy and grand about wandering its enormous emptiness. With the recent extension of the Hokuriku Shinkansen to Fukui, the temple is being slowly rediscovered as one of the country's most concentrated examples of single-patron sacred architecture.
highlights_en: A 17-meter Buddha and a 75-meter pagoda commissioned and funded entirely by one taxi-company founder in the late 1980s — possibly the most ambitious private-patron temple project in modern Japan
Approximately 1,281 smaller Buddhas distributed across precinct, cloister, and main hall
Visit before the crowds — for now, the Hokuriku Shinkansen has reopened access but the complex remains thrillingly underpopulated

name_en: Iwashita Shin-Shōga Museum (Pickled Ginger Theme Museum)
summary_en: The Iwashita Shin-Shōga Museum is what happens when a 19th-century pickle company decides to build the world's first museum dedicated entirely to its single most famous product: young pickled ginger. Opened in 2015 by Iwashita Foods in their home town of Tochigi, the museum is, top to bottom, ferociously pink: walls, ceilings, light fixtures, exhibits, cafeteria, mascot character (Iwashika-chan), even the in-house Shintō-style 'Ginger Shrine' where visitors can pray for ginger-related wishes. Permanent exhibits cover the history of ginger, the manufacturing process, vintage packaging from every decade since the 1950s, and a rotating series of crossover events with anime franchises, idol groups, and contemporary artists. The on-site cafe Pink Deli serves food in which essentially every dish includes new ginger as an ingredient — including ginger soft-serve, ginger curry, and ginger soda. Admission is free; photography is encouraged throughout; and the museum has become, against expectation, one of the surprise pilgrimage sites of provincial Japanese subculture tourism.
highlights_en: An entire museum, top to bottom in saturated pink, devoted to a single condiment — Iwashita's famous young pickled ginger
Includes a Shinto-style 'Ginger Shrine,' the mascot Iwashika-chan, and a cafe (Pink Deli) where every dish contains pickled ginger
Free admission, photography encouraged everywhere; against expectation, a serious pilgrimage site for provincial Japanese subculture

summary_en: On a low hill on the island of Shōdoshima in the Seto Inland Sea, a tall white standing Kannon — locally called the Shiawase Kannon, or 'Happiness Kannon' — rises above olive groves and small farms. The figure won first prize at a French international contest of architectural artworks (the so-called 'Arc Plus' competition), an unusual pedigree for a Japanese roadside Buddha and the reason the island's tourism agency cheerfully promotes the statue as 'the world's most beautiful Kannon.' Inside the statue's hollow body, an elevator carries visitors up through galleries holding 11,000 small votive Buddhas to a viewing chamber located inside the figure's chest, where windows look out across the Inland Sea toward Tonoshō Port and the famous Angel Road sandbar. Relics of the historical Buddha, donated from Sri Lanka, are enshrined in the chest gallery. At night, the figure is illuminated and visible from passing ferries — one of the small constellation of unexpected landmarks that makes Shōdoshima feel, at every turn, like a larger island than its map suggests.
highlights_en: A tall white Kannon on Shōdoshima, winner of the 'Arc Plus' international competition in France — the island's tourism agency calls her 'the world's most beautiful Kannon'
An elevator inside the figure passes 11,000 votive Buddhas before reaching a viewing chamber in the chest, with Buddha relics donated from Sri Lanka
Visible at night from passing ferries — one of the small constellation of unexpected landmarks that makes Shōdoshima feel larger than its map suggests

summary: 山形県鶴岡市大網（旧朝日村）の湯殿山総本寺・瀧水寺大日坊に祀られる即身仏・真如海上人（しんにょかいしょうにん、1687-1783）を拝観できる出羽三山・湯殿山信仰の中核寺院。大日坊公式・鶴岡市観光連盟の案内によれば、真如海上人は江戸中期に96歳で土中入定（生きながら土中に埋まり、念仏を唱えながら入定）した僧で、現在も即身仏として大日坊本堂に安置され、一般拝観が可能である。木食行（穀絶ち・五穀十穀断ち）・荒行・千日修行を経て肉身を浄化したのち、衆生救済の願をもって入定したとされ、湯殿山信仰の極致を体現する存在として全国の即身仏巡礼の中核に位置づけられる。庄内地方には複数の即身仏が現存し、本寺は最古級の真如海上人を祀る代表寺院である。
highlights: 江戸中期に96歳で土中入定した即身仏・真如海上人（1687-1783）を本堂に安置

from_kameyama: 車で約9時間（東名阪→新名神→名神→北陸道→日本海東北道→山形道経由）／新幹線＋特急いなほ＋バスで約7時間（亀山→名古屋→東京→新潟→鶴岡→大網方面）
summary_en: In the snow country of the Shōnai region, at the foot of the sacred Mount Yudono, the temple of Dainichibō has cared for the same congregation for over a thousand years — and at the center of its main hall sits its most famous member, the mummified body of the monk Shinnyokai-Shōnin, who entered sokushinbutsu ('living Buddhahood') in 1783 at the age of 96. The path that led him to this state is among the most extreme ascetic traditions in world religion: years of mokujikigyō (a diet of bark, roots, nuts, and pine needles, eliminating all grains) progressively dehydrated his body in life; long stretches of solitary meditation hollowed his metabolism; finally, while still alive, he was sealed underground in a stone chamber, breathing through a bamboo tube and chanting sutras until breath stopped. Three years later, his preserved body was disinterred and enshrined as a sokushinbutsu. He has been receiving visitors ever since. The temple treats him not as a curiosity but as a living priest still performing the same intercessory function he intended in 1783, and visitors are asked to behave as worshippers, not spectators. He is one of roughly sixteen sokushinbutsu still extant, the great majority of them in this small region of Yamagata.
highlights_en: The mummified body of Shinnyokai-Shōnin (1687-1783), who entered living Buddhahood through years of bark-and-pine-needle ascetic diet before being sealed underground while still alive
Dainichibō is one of the central temples of the Yudono-san mountain religion — Shōnai is the heartland of Japan's sokushinbutsu tradition
Treated as a living priest still performing intercession, not as a curiosity — visit as a worshipper, not a spectator

summary: 埼玉県飯能市上名栗の名栗山中に広がる、創設者・平沼弥太郎氏（1892-1979）が私財と発願で築いた約30万坪の私的霊場。本堂・大鐘楼・三蔵塔・玄奘三蔵塔・救世大観音・平和観音などの堂塔群が山中の参詣道・遊歩道沿いに点在し、頂上付近には高さ約33mの白い救世大観音像が立つ。鳥居観音公式・地域案内によれば、平沼氏は経済・宗教・教育活動を通じて世界平和と仏教興隆を願い、戦後復興期の昭和20〜40年代にかけて山中に堂塔群を建立した。山頂の救世大観音は胎内拝観・展望が可能（4〜11月の土日祝中心）で、奥武蔵の山並みを一望できる。個人発願による山中巨像・私的霊場という、戦後日本の宗教ランドマーク文化の代表事例の一つ。

from_kameyama: 車で約430分（東名阪→新東名→首都圏中央連絡道→飯能・名栗方面）／新幹線＋私鉄＋バスで約5時間（亀山→名古屋→東京→西武池袋線飯能駅→名栗方面バス）
summary_en: In the Naguri mountains north of Hannō, a single Saitama businessman named Hiranuma Yatarō (1892-1979) spent decades of the postwar reconstruction era building his own private 300,000-tsubo (roughly 100-hectare) Buddhist mountain. The Torii Kannon complex includes a main hall, a great bell tower, a 13-story pagoda dedicated to the relics of the 7th-century Chinese monk Xuanzang (whose travels to India inspired Journey to the West), a 33-meter white Kannon of Salvation on the summit, a Peace Kannon, a Shaka Buddha image, and a network of pilgrimage paths winding among them all. Most of the structures are open during the warmer months (April-November, weekends and holidays); the summit Kannon contains an interior elevator and a viewing platform that overlooks the rolling Oku-Musashi hills. The project is somewhere between religious devotion and personal monument-building on an unimaginable scale — a postwar layman's act of faith expressed in concrete and bronze across a hundred hectares of forest. Visiting on foot, with the right amount of time, is the closest most people will come to entering one person's actual mental landscape.
highlights_en: A 100-hectare private Buddhist mountain sanctuary built across the postwar decades by a single businessman, Hiranuma Yatarō
Centerpieces include a 33-meter white Kannon on the summit (with interior elevator and viewing platform) and a 13-story pagoda for relics of Xuanzang, the Chinese pilgrim of Journey to the West
Open weekends and holidays April-November — the closest most visitors will come to walking through one person's actual mental landscape

summary: 奈良県天理市柳本町、山の辺の道沿いに佇む高野山真言宗の古刹・長岳寺（弘法大師空海開創と伝わる）が、毎年10月23日〜11月30日の約40日間にわたり特別公開する九幅構成の極楽地獄絵を中心とする秋季の地獄絵開帳行事。長岳寺公式・奈良県観光公式の案内によれば、本絵は江戸時代の作と伝わる九幅一具の大幅で、十王裁判・三途の川・奪衣婆（だつえば）・八大地獄（等活・黒縄・衆合・叫喚・大叫喚・焦熱・大焦熱・無間）・極楽浄土などの六道思想・地獄観・浄土観を絵画化したもの。期間中の土日祝13:00頃を中心に住職による絵解き説法が行われ、参詣者は古寺の伽藍と紅葉の中で、近世以前の日本人が地獄・極楽・因果応報をどう視覚化してきたかを直接体験できる。山の辺の道・大和古寺巡礼の一端として、地獄絵・絵解き文化・六道思想の現代的継承を示す稀有な秋季公開行事。

from_kameyama: 車で約1時間40分（東名阪・名阪国道・西名阪→天理東IC→山の辺の道方面）／JRで約2時間（亀山→加茂→奈良→桜井線柳本駅→徒歩約20分）
summary_en: For about forty days each autumn (October 23 to November 30), the temple of Chōgaku-ji on the Yamanobe-no-Michi — Japan's oldest documented road — unrolls a set of nine large painted scrolls in its main hall. The Daijigokue, or Great Hell Scrolls, date from the Edo period and depict, with extraordinary visual force, the full Buddhist cosmology of postmortem judgment: the Ten Kings of Hell presiding over their tribunals, the old hag Datsue-ba stripping the dead at the Sanzu River, the Eight Great Hells (boiling, knife-edged, crushing, screaming, twice-screaming, scorching, twice-scorching, and the endless avici), and finally the Pure Land of Amida. On weekend afternoons during the opening, the head priest performs etoki — the medieval Japanese practice of 'picture-explaining,' walking visitors through the scrolls as a vivid morality drama. Etoki is a living oral tradition that has nearly died out elsewhere; here it continues in the soft autumn light, with the same scrolls that Edo-period villagers stared at five generations ago. Combine with a walk along the Yamanobe-no-Michi past the imperial tumuli for one of Nara's most concentrated days of folk Buddhism.
highlights_en: Nine Edo-period painted scrolls depicting the Buddhist hells and the Pure Land — unrolled only during the autumn opening, October 23 to November 30
Live etoki ('picture-explaining') by the head priest on weekend afternoons — a medieval oral tradition still working in 21st-century Nara
Combine with a walk on the Yamanobe-no-Michi past ancient imperial tumuli for one of Nara's most concentrated days of folk Buddhism

name: 湯殿山 注連寺 即身仏鉄門海上人

status: 現存・拝観可（不定休・要事前確認、団体は要相談）

summary: 山形県鶴岡市大網（旧朝日村）、湯殿山の里宮口に位置する真言宗智山派の古刹・注連寺（ちゅうれんじ）に祀られる即身仏・鉄門海上人（てつもんかいしょうにん、1759-1829）を拝観できる出羽三山・湯殿山信仰の中核寺院の一つ。注連寺公式・鶴岡市観光連盟の案内によれば、鉄門海上人は江戸後期の湯殿山の行者で、自らの片眼を奉納し木食行（穀絶ち・五穀十穀断ち）・荒行・千日修行を経て、衆生救済の願をもって土中入定（生きながら土中に埋まり念仏を唱えながら入定）した僧として、現在も即身仏として注連寺本堂に安置され、一般拝観が可能である。本寺は森敦『月山』の舞台としても知られ、雪深い庄内の山あいの古刹に湯殿山信仰の極致と日本近代文学が重なる、出羽三山・庄内即身仏巡礼の代表的な目的地の一つ。
highlights: 江戸後期に土中入定した即身仏・鉄門海上人（1759-1829）を本堂に安置
出羽三山・湯殿山信仰の中核寺院、真言宗智山派の古刹・注連寺
森敦『月山』の舞台としても知られる雪深い庄内の山あいの古刹
from_kameyama: 車で約9時間（東名阪→新名神→名神→北陸道→日本海東北道→山形道経由）／新幹線＋特急いなほ＋バスで約7時間（亀山→名古屋→東京→新潟→鶴岡→大網方面）
summary_en: A short walk from Dainichibō, in the same snow-bound valley below sacred Mount Yudono, the temple of Churen-ji holds another sokushinbutsu — a 'living Buddha' — named Tetsumonkai-Shōnin, who entered self-mummification in 1829. His story is unusual even in this unusual tradition: as a young man he is said to have killed two samurai officials in a dispute, and in atonement first cut out one of his own eyes and offered it as a votive sacrifice, then spent the rest of his life in extreme ascetic practice — bark-and-needle diet, thousand-day meditations, ritual sealings — before finally being buried alive in a stone chamber and emerging three years later as a sokushinbutsu. Churen-ji is also famous in modern Japanese literature as the setting of Mori Atsushi's novel Gassan (which won the Akutagawa Prize in 1974), and reading the novel before visiting deepens the experience of approaching this small wooden temple buried under several meters of winter snow. The temple is currently a working center of Shingon Buddhism; treat Tetsumonkai-Shōnin as a present-day teacher, not a museum exhibit.
highlights_en: The mummified body of Tetsumonkai-Shōnin (1759-1829), who cut out his own eye in atonement before completing the long ascetic process toward self-mummification
Setting of Mori Atsushi's Akutagawa Prize-winning novel Gassan — reading the novel before visiting transforms the experience of the temple
Active Shingon temple in deep Shōnai snow country; Tetsumonkai-Shōnin is treated as a present-day teacher, not a museum exhibit

summary: 大阪市平野区平野本町、大阪市内最古級の聖徳太子開創伝承を持つ高野山真言宗の古刹・全興寺の境内に併設された、地獄表現と六道思想を体験的に見せる小堂「地獄堂」を中核とする民間信仰・地獄観のテーマ拠点。全興寺公式の案内によれば、地獄堂内には等身大の閻魔大王像・奪衣婆（だつえば）・地獄の鬼などが安置され、参拝者は「地獄の釜の音」を聞く装置、極楽度・地獄度を判定するチェックボックス、六道輪廻を体感する仏間など、近世以来の地獄・極楽観を現代的に翻案した体験装置で六道思想に触れることができる。境内には「ほとけのくに（仏の国）」「駄菓子屋さん博物館」など多彩な小展示も併設され、寺院資料館とは異なる庶民信仰と地獄表現の体験装置として、大阪の隠れたB級スポット・寺院体験施設の代表格として親しまれてきた。
highlights: 等身大の閻魔大王像・奪衣婆・地獄の鬼を安置する体験型の地獄堂
「地獄の釜の音」装置・極楽度地獄度チェック・六道輪廻体感装置を備える
聖徳太子開創伝承を持つ大阪市内最古級の高野山真言宗古刹・全興寺の境内施設
from_kameyama: 車で約2時間（東名阪→新名神→近畿道→阪神高速・松原線経由）／JR・近鉄・地下鉄で約2時間30分（亀山→名古屋→新大阪→大阪→地下鉄谷町線平野駅徒歩約5分）
summary_en: In the medieval merchant district of Hirano, in southeastern Ōsaka, one of the city's oldest temples — Senkō-ji, traditionally said to have been founded by Prince Shōtoku in the 7th century — has discreetly turned itself into one of Japan's most playful folk-Buddhist experience halls. Within its small precinct, a building called the Hell Hall (Jigokudō) contains life-sized statues of King Yama (the lord of the dead), the old hag Datsue-ba (who strips the dead of their clothes at the river of judgment), and a chorus of attendant demons. Visitors press buttons to hear 'the sound of the cauldrons of hell,' answer interactive questionnaires that score their personal hell-potential and paradise-potential, and pass through a small meditation room called the Realm of the Six Paths. Other attractions on the same precinct include the Land of Buddhas (Hotoke-no-Kuni) and a vintage candy-store museum. The whole compound is run as a children's-friendly introduction to medieval Buddhist cosmology and folk religion, and it works: the kids enjoy themselves, and the adults leave more thoughtful than they expected.
highlights_en: A Hell Hall with life-sized King Yama, Datsue-ba and chorus of demons, plus push-button sound effects and an interactive 'hell potential' quiz
Senkō-ji is traditionally founded by Prince Shōtoku in the 7th century — one of the oldest temples in Ōsaka
Children's-friendly introduction to medieval Buddhist cosmology that quietly succeeds with adults as well

summary: 石川県金沢市笠市町、金沢駅にほど近い浄土真宗本願寺派の寺院・照円寺で、毎年春と秋の彼岸期に行われる「地獄極楽絵図ご開帳」を中心とする寺宝公開行事。照円寺公式・石川県観光公式の案内によれば、本寺には江戸時代より伝わる地獄極楽絵図一具が伝来し、春・秋の彼岸の特別公開期間中に本堂内で参拝者に披露される。絵図には八種の地獄、六道輪廻、十種の極楽浄土などが描かれ、近世以前の日本人が地獄・極楽・因果応報をどう視覚化してきたかを一堂に見ることができる。金沢駅近接の市街地寺院でありながら、王道観光ルート（金沢城・兼六園・東茶屋街・近江町市場）から外れた、六道思想・地獄絵の背景を持つ短期公開型の文化財公開行事として、奈良・長岳寺の大地獄絵開帳などと並ぶ、近世仏教来世観の現代的継承を示す稀有な事例。
highlights: 江戸時代より伝わる地獄極楽絵図一具を春秋の彼岸期に本堂で特別公開
八種の地獄・六道輪廻・十種の極楽浄土を一堂に描く近世仏教来世観の絵画化
金沢駅近接の都市部寺院での短期公開型寺宝公開行事
from_kameyama: 車で約4時間30分（東名阪→新名神→名神→北陸道→金沢西IC経由）／新幹線で約3時間（亀山→名古屋→米原・敦賀経由・北陸新幹線金沢駅徒歩約10分）
summary_en: A ten-minute walk from Kanazawa Station, in a quiet neighborhood mostly missed by tourists, the Jōdo Shinshū temple Shōen-ji holds a set of Edo-period painted scrolls depicting the Eight Great Hells, the Six Paths of rebirth, and ten paradises of the Pure Land. The scrolls are unrolled in the main hall only during the higan equinox weeks of spring and autumn — short windows that coincide with the traditional Buddhist seasons of remembering the dead — and at all other times remain rolled and stored. The visit is best treated as a small pilgrimage rather than a museum stop: the temple is working, the scrolls are being exhibited inside an actual ritual cycle, and the head priest is generally available to walk through the imagery for visitors who arrive with sincere interest. Like the Chōgaku-ji opening in Nara, this is a chance to see what Edo-period villagers actually saw when their parish priest opened the hell paintings on the equinox afternoon: a vivid, terrifying, beautifully composed argument for behaving well in this life.
highlights_en: Edo-period painted scrolls of the Eight Hells, the Six Paths and ten paradises — opened only during the spring and autumn higan equinox weeks
An active Jōdo Shinshū temple ten minutes from Kanazawa Station, almost entirely off the main tourist circuit
A small pilgrimage rather than a museum stop — best approached with sincere interest and a willingness to ask the head priest for an explanation

summary: 徳島県阿南市加茂町に鎮座する、別名『猫神さん』として信仰される神社。お松権現社公式の由緒によれば、江戸前期の阿波・加茂村で、不当な代官の取り立てに苦しんだ村の女性『お松』が直訴の末に処刑され、その無念を晴らすために飼い猫が怪猫となって代官の家を災いに陥れたとの伝承が伝わる。後に村人がお松を権現として祀り、飼い猫の霊をも合祀したのが当社の縁起とされ、近世以来『猫神さん』として、訴訟・勝負事・願掛けの神として信仰されてきた。社殿の周囲には参拝者が奉納した約一万体ともいわれる招き猫が並び、参道には狛犬ならぬ『狛猫』が鎮座する。怪猫伝承・猫神信仰・大量の招き猫奉納というB級スポット的要素を持つ徳島県の代表的な民俗信仰の場の一つ。
highlights: 江戸前期のお松直訴伝承と飼い猫の怪猫伝承を背景とする『猫神さん』
社殿周囲に奉納された約一万体ともいわれる招き猫群
狛犬ならぬ『狛猫』が守る猫神信仰の社
from_kameyama: 車で約4時間（東名阪→新名神→神戸淡路鳴門自動車道→徳島道→徳島南部・那賀方面）／新幹線＋特急で約5時間（亀山→新大阪→新神戸→高速バスまたは特急『うずしお』徳島駅→JR牟岐線阿南駅→タクシー）
summary_en: In the village of Kamo, in coastal Anan, a small Shinto shrine has, over four centuries, absorbed approximately ten thousand maneki-neko statues offered by petitioners praying for victory in legal disputes, court cases, and contests of every kind. Omatsu Gongen-sha is dedicated to a 17th-century village woman named Omatsu, who, according to local legend, attempted to appeal to higher authorities against a corrupt local magistrate, was instead executed for her trouble, and whose pet cat — moved by her death — transformed into a vengeful spirit-cat (bakeneko) and brought ruin to the magistrate's household. The villagers, recognizing both forces, enshrined Omatsu and the cat together as deities of justice and grievance. Today the shrine is the only one in Japan where koma-neko (guardian cats) replace the usual koma-inu (guardian dogs) at the entrance, and where the steps to the inner shrine are lined floor-to-rafters with cat figurines left by petitioners, many of them lawyers, plaintiffs, and gamblers. The story is sad, the iconography is wild, and the cumulative visual impact is unlike anything else in the country.
highlights_en: Approximately 10,000 maneki-neko statues offered by petitioners seeking victory in lawsuits, contests and gambles
The only shrine in Japan where koma-neko (guardian cats) replace the standard koma-inu (guardian dogs) at the entrance
Foundation legend: a 17th-century village woman wrongfully executed by a corrupt magistrate, avenged by her transformed pet cat

summary: 愛媛県宇和島市藤江に鎮座する多賀神社の境内に併設される性信仰の文化資料館で、別名『凸凹神堂』として知られる。宇和島市観光協会・愛媛県観光物産協会の案内によれば、本社は古来より延命栄寿・治病・子授け・健康の霊験で知られる多賀大神を祀り、参拝者の奉納・寄贈による性に関する民俗資料・美術工芸品を収蔵・展示する『性文化財資料館』を併設している。多賀大神への子授け・夫婦和合・無病息災の信仰を背景に、近世以来の春画・性的造形物・性に関わる民具・郷土玩具などが体系的に収蔵されており、性を子授け・繁栄・健康の象徴として捉える日本の民俗信仰の文脈で位置付けられる施設として知られる。未成年（18歳未満）は神堂・資料館への入館不可。

境内に併設される性文化財資料館『凸凹神堂』
性を子授け・繁栄・健康の象徴として捉える日本の民俗信仰の収蔵展示

name_en: Taga Shrine Dekoboko Shindō (Phallic Folk Museum)
summary_en: In the coastal city of Uwajima, on Shikoku's western edge, a branch shrine of the great Taga Shrine of Ōmi maintains an adjoining 'Folk Cultural Property Museum' (Dekoboko Shindō, literally 'Hall of the Uneven Gods') devoted to the folk-religious symbolism of sexuality. The collection has been built up over generations by votive donation: Edo-period shunga (erotic woodblock prints) and ukiyo-e, carved wooden and stone phalli and vulvae, fertility folk-art from across Japan, ema votive plaques inscribed with prayers for conception and matrimonial harmony, and curiosities from foreign sex-cultures collected by previous priests. Taga is one of the few major shrines in Japan whose stated purview includes longevity, healing, childbirth, and marital fertility, and the museum exists to make that traditional purview material rather than coy. Entry is restricted to visitors over 18; the curation is matter-of-fact and the framing is anthropological rather than titillating. For visitors interested in the older Japanese view of human reproduction as religious rather than embarrassing, the Dekoboko Shindō is among the most concentrated collections in the country.
highlights_en: An adjoining museum at a major Taga Shrine branch — Edo-period shunga, carved phalli, fertility ema, and an extensive folk-art collection
Documenting the older Japanese view of human reproduction as a religious matter governed by Taga's longevity-and-fertility deities
Entry restricted to visitors over 18; framing is anthropological rather than titillating, with detailed Japanese-language labeling

summary: 島根県出雲市唐川町の山中に鎮座する古社。出雲市・島根県神社庁の案内によれば、出雲国風土記・延喜式神名帳にも見える由緒ある神社で、祭神は素盞嗚命（スサノオノミコト）。素盞嗚命が新羅から船で渡来した際に乗ってきたとされる岩船が境内裏にあるという伝承や、新羅由来の鉄・釜（竈）の文化と結び付くと伝わる古社。参道は約300段の石段と、本殿手前の幅45cmほどの巨大な岩の隙間を抜けて参拝する険しい構造で知られ、舗装されておらず携帯電話の電波もほぼ届かない山中の社として、岩の隙間参道・鉄文化伝承・素盞嗚命信仰を主軸に位置付けられる。
highlights: 出雲国風土記・延喜式神名帳に記される素盞嗚命を祀る古社
本殿手前の幅45cm程度の巨大な岩の隙間を抜けて参拝する険しい参道
新羅由来の鉄・竈（かま）の文化と結び付くと伝わる山中の古社
from_kameyama: 車で約6時間（東名阪→新名神→中国道→米子道・米子自動車道→山陰道→出雲ICまたは斐川IC経由・山道走行）／新幹線＋特急で約7時間（亀山→新大阪→新幹線『さくら』『みずほ』岡山→特急『やくも』出雲市駅→タクシー・レンタカーで唐川町方面）。最寄り駅から韓竈神社登り口まではタクシー・レンタカーが必須で、登り口から本殿までは徒歩約30〜60分（往復約1〜2時間）の山道。
summary_en: Up a mountain road in the woods of Karakawa-chō, outside Izumo, a small shrine of striking antiquity is recorded in both the 733 Izumo no Kuni Fudoki and the 927 Engishiki — making it one of the oldest documented shrines in Japan. Karakama Shrine is dedicated to Susanoo-no-Mikoto, the storm god of Japanese myth, who is said to have arrived from the kingdom of Silla on the Korean Peninsula on a stone boat that is identified with a boulder still behind the shrine. The name 'Karakama' means 'Korean cauldron,' a reference to the iron-and-cauldron technology associated with Silla and absorbed into the deep iron-smelting culture of Izumo. To reach the main shrine, pilgrims climb about three hundred uneven stone steps, and then — just before the shrine itself — squeeze through a 45-centimeter gap between two huge boulders, an authentic test of body and intent. No cell signal, no signage, no concessions to the casual visitor; the mountain forest and the gap in the rock are the entire experience.
highlights_en: Documented in the 733 Izumo Fudoki and 927 Engishiki — one of the oldest documented shrines in Japan
To reach the inner shrine, pilgrims must squeeze through a 45-centimeter gap between two huge boulders
Dedicated to Susanoo, who according to legend arrived from Silla on a stone boat — a deep link between Izumo iron culture and the Korean Peninsula

summary: 富士山麓の洞穴と富士講碑群からなる信仰遺跡。人穴は中世から富士山の神・浅間大菩薩の住まいとされ、冥界へ通じる洞穴として語られてきた。富士講の開祖とされる長谷川角行の修行地とも伝えられ、入口周辺には巡礼者が残した200基以上の石碑が並ぶ。洞窟、冥界伝承、講碑群が一体になった、富士信仰の異界性を感じられる場所。
highlights: 冥界往還の伝承を持つ富士山麓の洞穴
富士講の巡礼者が残した200基以上の石碑群
長谷川角行の修行地とされる富士信仰の重要地点
from_kameyama: 車で約230分（新名神→伊勢湾岸道→新東名）
summary_en: On the western slopes of Mount Fuji, a lava cave called Hitoana ('the Human Hole') has been understood since at least the 13th century as a literal entrance to the realm of the kami of Fuji — the great Sengen Daibosatsu — and as a portal between the world of the living and the world of the dead. In the early 17th century, the cave became the meditation retreat of Hasegawa Kakugyō, the founder of the Fujikō pilgrim associations, who is said to have spent extended periods inside in austere training. From the 18th century onward, as the Fujikō movement spread among Edo townsfolk, ordinary pilgrims began leaving inscribed stone stelae outside the cave entrance to mark their visits, their prayers, and their dead. More than two hundred such stelae remain today, dating from across two centuries of working-class religious practice, set in a small ravine around the cave mouth. The site is administered by Fujinomiya City; access to the cave interior is by reservation through the local museum, and the surface stelae can be visited freely outside winter.
highlights_en: A lava cave understood since the 13th century as an entrance to the realm of Fuji's deity — and as a portal to the world of the dead
Meditation retreat of Hasegawa Kakugyō, the 17th-century founder of the Fujikō pilgrim associations
Over 200 inscribed stone stelae left by Fujikō pilgrims across two centuries, clustered in the ravine around the cave mouth

from_kameyama: 車で約330分（新名神→伊勢湾岸道→新東名→東京湾アクアライン）
summary_en: On a high bluff overlooking Tōkyō Bay, in Futtsu City on the Bōsō Peninsula, a 56-meter white standing Kannon faces north toward Tōkyō, her right hand raised in benediction over the water. Built in 1961 as a private monument to peace, the Tōkyō Bay Kannon predates the great wave of 100-meter-plus Buddhas that followed in the 1980s and 90s, and has the more modest, slightly battered scale of an earlier postwar era. Inside, a spiral staircase climbs 324 steps through 20 floors of small votive halls and tableaux — Seven Lucky Gods, miniature Buddhas, hand-painted devotional images — before emerging onto a small open-air observation crown at the level of the Kannon's tiara, where visitors look out across Tōkyō Bay toward the skyline of the capital. The experience is unmistakably Shōwa-era — handpainted murals on the staircase walls, mid-century concrete textures, the smell of old incense — and the climb is steep and unair-conditioned, with the result that emerging into the open sea breeze at the top feels like a small physical reward.
highlights_en: A 56-meter white Kannon on a Bōsō Peninsula bluff overlooking Tōkyō Bay — completed in 1961, predating the giant-Buddha boom of later decades
324-step interior staircase passes through 20 floors of small votive halls before emerging onto an open-air crown at the level of the Kannon's tiara
Distinctly Shōwa-era atmosphere — handpainted murals, mid-century concrete, the smell of old incense — and a hard-earned view across the bay to the Tōkyō skyline

from_kameyama: 車で約240分（新名神→伊勢湾岸道→新東名→東富士五湖道路）
name_en: Funatsu Tainai Jukei (Womb-Cave Lava Tree Mold)
summary_en: When lava from one of Mount Fuji's eruptions engulfed a living tree, the wood eventually burned away and left behind a long, sinuous cavity in the cooled stone — a tree-shaped tunnel in the lava itself. At Funatsu, this kind of cavity was reinterpreted by Fujikō pilgrims as a 'tainai' (womb), and entering and exiting through the narrow opening became a ritual of symbolic rebirth, performed by pilgrims on the day before climbing Mount Fuji to purify the body and start the ascent anew. The interior walls are striated with rib-like patterns formed where bark once stood; the local pilgrims interpreted these as the literal ribcage of the mother goddess Sengen Daibosatsu, completing the womb metaphor with anatomical specificity. The cave is a designated Natural Monument, administered today by Fujikawaguchiko Town; admission is modest, the cave is electrically lit but very narrow, and the experience of squeezing through the constriction and emerging into open air is one of the most unforgettable small rituals available on the Fuji circuit.
highlights_en: A natural lava tree-mold cave reinterpreted by Fujikō pilgrims as a 'womb' — passing through is a ritual of symbolic rebirth before climbing Fuji
Rib-like patterns on the interior walls were understood as the literal ribcage of the mother goddess Sengen Daibosatsu
Designated Natural Monument, electrically lit but very narrow — squeezing through and emerging into open air is the entire ritual

name_en: Mount Osore (Reijō Osorezan, Sacred Mountain of the Dead)
summary_en: On the Shimokita Peninsula at the northern tip of Honshū, an active volcanic caldera holds one of Japan's three most sacred mountains for the dead. Mount Osore is a yellow-gray landscape of sulfur vents, bubbling hot springs, and bone-white pumice fields, ringed by the milky-blue Lake Usori, and Buddhist tradition has overlaid these features so completely with the geography of the afterlife that the walk through them functions as a kind of dress rehearsal for one's own dying. A small bridge crossing a stream is identified as the Sanzu River, the boundary that the dead must cross. Beyond it, the Eight Great Hells unfold across the actual volcanic terrain: rocks become Datsue-ba and the demons of judgment, sulfur vents become hell-mouths, and the cracked moonscape near the central temple is the Sai-no-Kawara, the dry riverbed where the souls of dead children stack stones in penance — except here the stones really are stacked, in vast quiet drifts, by living parents and grandparents memorializing real losses. Pinwheels turn in the wind beside them. The mountain is open from May to October; treat the entire experience as funerary rather than touristic.
highlights_en: An active volcanic caldera mapped point-for-point onto the Buddhist landscape of the afterlife — Sanzu River, Eight Hells, Sai-no-Kawara, Pure Land beach
Stones stacked by grieving parents at the Sai-no-Kawara turn the abstract folk Buddhism of dying-children-and-stones into something physically present
Open May to October only — closed for snow the rest of the year — and is to be treated as a working funerary site, not as a tourism curiosity

name: 成田山 久留米分院 明王寺 地獄館

summary: 久留米成田山の境内にある、地獄表現を常設展示する施設。黒縄地獄、大叫喚地獄、焦熱地獄、阿鼻地獄など、仏教的な戒めを視覚的に見せる構成で、巨大な救世慈母大観音像、平和大仏塔極楽殿、孝行洞窟と合わせて巡る。宗教施設内の教化展示でありながら、強い造形性とB級性を併せ持つ。
highlights: 黒縄地獄、焦熱地獄、阿鼻地獄などを視覚化した常設展示
救世慈母大観音像や平和大仏塔と一体で巡れる構成

from_kameyama: 車で約480分（新名神→山陽道→九州道）／新幹線＋バスで約5時間30分
name_en: Narita-san Kurume Branch Temple Jigokukan (Hell Hall)
summary_en: On the outskirts of Kurume, in Fukuoka Prefecture, a regional branch of the famous Narita-san temple complex maintains one of the more theatrically committed hell experiences in Japanese Buddhism. The Jigokukan ('Hell Hall') is a walk-through diorama of the Eight Great Hells of Buddhist cosmology — Black Rope, Crushing, Screaming, Twice-Screaming, Scorching, Twice-Scorching, Reviving, and the endless avici — each rendered as a separate room with life-sized figures of demons inflicting period-appropriate torments on the damned, accompanied by sound effects and selective lighting that genuinely raise the heart rate. The Hell Hall is positioned within a larger pilgrimage circuit that includes a vast Merciful Mother Kannon, a Peace Pagoda containing a Pure Land hall, and a Filial Piety Cave — moving the visitor through fear, mercy, and gratitude in roughly that order. The intent is moral instruction in the medieval Japanese pattern: see the hells in physical detail, then experience the consolation of paradise. Allow an hour for the full circuit and bring children old enough to know that the demons are not real.
highlights_en: A walk-through diorama of the Eight Great Hells with life-sized demons and selective lighting — moral instruction in the medieval Japanese pattern
Hell is only the first stop: the full circuit moves through a Merciful Mother Kannon, a Peace Pagoda Pure Land hall, and a Filial Piety Cave
Allow an hour for the complete circuit; the demons are intense, so bring children old enough to know they aren't real

from_kameyama: 車で約480分（新名神→山陽道→中国道→九州道→東九州道）
summary_en: On the site of the former Keisho-ji temple in Ajimu, Usa City, a late-Edo-period subterranean pilgrimage walks visitors through a forty-meter Hell tunnel and a thirty-meter Paradise tunnel carved into the hillside. Inside, statues of Enma the Judge, the ox-headed and horse-headed wardens, the old hag who strips the dead at the River of Three Crossings, the Pool of Blood, and the Thirteen Buddhas are arranged so that the visitor physically experiences the journey from damnation to salvation. The walk ends with a vertical shaft that must be climbed by chain to reach paradise — a deliberately demanding finish that turned the route into a popular form of folk Buddhist instruction in the nineteenth century. The site is unstaffed, the lighting is minimal, and a flashlight is essential.
highlights_en: A walk-through subterranean Hell-and-Paradise pilgrimage built in the late Edo period for popular Buddhist instruction
Life-sized figures of Enma, the ox-headed and horse-headed wardens, the river-crossing hag, and the Pool of Blood line the Hell tunnel
The route ends with a vertical chain-climb up a natural shaft into 'paradise' — bring a flashlight and grippy shoes

summary_en: Shokan-ji, a Kegon-school temple in the fishing town of Mugi on Tokushima's Pacific coast, maintains a walk-through rendering of the Eight Great Hells of Buddhist cosmology — Reviving, Black Rope, Crushing, Screaming, Twice-Screaming, Scorching, Twice-Scorching, and the endless avici. The hell circuit is only open from noon until four in the afternoon, separate from the main grounds, and an admission fee is charged. The atmosphere is closer to a temple's pedagogical hall than to a roadside curiosity: the figures are explicitly framed as a teaching about karma and consequence, not a haunted-house attraction. Hours can shift with weather and staffing, so call ahead.
highlights_en: A walk-through rendering of the Eight Great Hells inside a working Kegon-school temple complex

summary_en: On the lava skirts of Mount Fuji's eastern flank, an approximately sixty-eight-meter lava tube has been read since at least the Edo period as the interior of a human body and consecrated as a shrine. Visitors enter Otainai Seikoen, a small park built around the tube, and then crawl, stoop, and squeeze through the tunnel as if traversing a womb, emerging on the far side where the Tainai Shrine — long associated with safe childbirth — is enshrined. The site is one of several 'womb caves' scattered across the foot of Fuji, where natural lava geography and folk beliefs about rebirth and fertility have layered onto each other. Closed-toe shoes and a willingness to get a little muddy are non-negotiable.
highlights_en: A sixty-eight-meter lava tube on Mount Fuji's eastern flank, consecrated as the interior of a human body
Visitors traverse the cave as if through a womb, emerging at the Tainai Shrine of safe childbirth

summary_en: Mara Kannon is a small wayside shrine in the hot-spring district of Tawarayama, Nagato, where the local story begins in tragedy. When the warlord Ouchi Yoshitaka was overthrown in 1551, his young heir was reportedly disguised as a girl and smuggled into hiding here, only to be discovered, captured, and killed. The shrine began as a memorial for the boy, and over the centuries the offerings — carved wooden phalluses left by parishioners — took on a second life as petitions for fertility, safe childbirth, marital harmony, and good matches. The site is small, free, and open-air, and a local memorial service is still held annually. It is best understood not as a roadside curiosity but as a layering of mourning, lineage, and fertility belief — and depicted with that in mind.
highlights_en: A small shrine memorializing the killed heir of the Ouchi clan, now layered with fertility and matchmaking belief
Offerings are carved wooden phalluses left by parishioners — please photograph and write about it with respect
A local memorial service is still observed; the site is free, open-air, and treated seriously by neighbors

summary_en: Behind the modest hall of Oshiko Shrine in Takasago sits the Ishi-no-Hoden — the 'Stone Treasure Hall' — a single block of Tatsuyama tuff over five meters on a side, carved out of the surrounding bedrock but never fully detached from it. The stone is the shrine's object of worship, traditionally counted as one of the 'Three Wonders of Japan' alongside the salt cauldron of Shiogama Shrine and the heavenly halberd at Kirishima-Higashi Shrine. Because the base appears to float over the standing water in the quarry trench below, it is also called the 'floating stone.' The site is part of a nationally designated historic landmark covering both the worship space and the surrounding ancient quarries — a place where Kofun-era stoneworking, an unfinished monumental cut, and a still-active shrine sit on the same rock.
highlights_en: A five-meter monolith carved from living bedrock and never freed — the shrine's object of worship
Traditionally counted as one of the Three Wonders of Japan; locally known as the 'floating stone'
Part of a nationally designated historic site that joins active worship with ancient Tatsuyama-stone quarries

The site is treated here not as sexual spectacle but as a folk record of devotion and votive practice around the body and reproduction.
highlights_en: A temple of sexual faith preserving many dedicated phallic votives

summary_en: A shrine in Oguni revered for fertility, safe childbirth, child-rearing, and matchmaking. In an octagonal sub-shrine beside the main hall, two yin-yang megaliths are enshrined — the male stone 'Kanamaru Taro' and the female stone 'Tanesawa Hanako' — bringing together the faith in musubi (creative life force) and the symbolism of sex and reproduction. The site is presented as a continuing tradition of Tohoku fertility worship rather than as mere curiosity.
highlights_en: Yin-yang megaliths enshrined as the male stone 'Kanamaru Taro' and the female stone 'Tanesawa Hanako'
A shrine of fertility, safe childbirth, and child-rearing rooted in musubi (life-creating) belief

summary_en: A field of megaliths spreading behind Itsukushima Shrine in Nagusa, Ashikaga. The city's official page describes the cluster as a nationally rare example of 'onion-skin' weathering in granite, with notable features including the Osonaeishi — over 11 meters tall and more than 30 meters in circumference, traditionally used for a 'rebirth' passage — and the Benkei no Tewari-ishi (Benkei's split stone). The site combines natural wonder with iwakura-style stone worship.
highlights_en: The Osonaeishi — over 11 m tall and 30+ m in circumference, used for a 'rebirth' passage
A nationally rare granite outcrop showing classic 'onion-skin' spheroidal weathering
An iwakura-style sacred space deep in the precincts of Itsukushima Shrine

name: 霊巌寺の奇岩
name_kana: れいがんじのきがん
address: 福岡県八女市黒木町笠原9731
summary: 八女市黒木町の霊巌寺境内にそびえる県指定天然記念物の奇岩群。市公式は、座禅岩、女岩、仲人岩、仏法岩、剣岩、犬岩、猫岩といった名を挙げ、なかでも高さ12m余の男岩は日本最大級と説明する。寺院・修験・奇岩名・性象徴が重なり、資料館色のない自然信仰系候補として強い。
highlights: 座禅岩、女岩、仲人岩、剣岩、犬岩、猫岩など名を持つ奇岩群
高さ12m余の男岩は日本最大級とされる
八女茶発祥地の古刹と修験的な霊地性が重なる
from_kameyama: 車で約520分（新名神→山陽道→九州道）
summary_en: Within the precincts of Reigan-ji in Kuroki-machi, Yame, stand a prefecturally designated cluster of strange rocks. The city's official page names individual stones — Zazen-iwa (Meditation Rock), Onna-iwa (Woman Rock), Nakodo-iwa (Matchmaker Rock), Buppo-iwa, Tsurugi-iwa (Sword Rock), Inu-iwa (Dog Rock), Neko-iwa (Cat Rock) — and notes that the Otoko-iwa (Man Rock), over 12 m tall, is said to be among the largest in Japan. Temple, Shugendo ascetic associations, named formations, and sexual symbolism overlap in a strong nature-faith candidate without a museum framing.
highlights_en: Named formations including Zazen, Woman, Matchmaker, Sword, Dog, and Cat Rocks
The Otoko-iwa (Man Rock), over 12 m tall, said to be among the largest in Japan
An ancient temple in the birthplace of Yame tea, overlapping with a Shugendo sacred site

name: 耕三寺 千佛洞地獄峡
name_kana: こうさんじ せんぶつどうじごくきょう
address: 広島県尾道市瀬戸田町瀬戸田553-2
summary: 瀬戸田の耕三寺にある全長350mの洞窟型地獄・極楽表現。公式ページでは、源信『往生要集』を視覚で体感するため、富士山の溶岩と浅間山の焼石で岩組みし、約千体の石仏と彫刻絵で十界の場面を表したものと説明される。資料館ではなく『地獄洞窟』として切り出すと、全国版の地獄系スポットに合う。
highlights: 横洞350m・最深部15mの洞窟型地獄極楽空間
約千体の石仏と彫刻絵で十界を表現
富士山溶岩と浅間山焼石で岩組みされた人工洞窟
from_kameyama: 車で約300分（新名神→山陽道→しまなみ海道）
name_en: Kosan-ji — Senbutsudo Jigokukyo (Cave of a Thousand Buddhas)
summary_en: Inside Kosan-ji on Setoda, a 350-meter cave passage stages the hells and paradises of Buddhist cosmology in physical form. The temple's official page describes how, to let visitors experience Genshin's Ojo-Yoshu through sight, the cavern was built up with lava from Mt. Fuji and burnt stones from Mt. Asama, with roughly a thousand stone Buddhas and relief carvings enacting the Ten Realms. Rather than framing the whole complex as a museum, the listing focuses on this hell-cavern as a singular grotto installation.
highlights_en: A 350-meter horizontal cave passage of hells and paradises, descending up to 15 m
Roughly a thousand stone Buddhas and relief carvings depicting the Ten Realms
Cavern walls built from Mt. Fuji lava and Mt. Asama burnt stone

name: 牛伏寺 地獄めぐり回廊
name_kana: ごふくじ じごくめぐりかいろう
address: 長野県松本市内田2573
hours: 受付時間9:00〜17:00目安、地獄回廊は要確認
summary: 松本市内田の古刹・牛伏寺にある地獄めぐり回廊。ローカル報道では、約200年前から伝わる木像で地獄の様子を表現する回廊が2022年に完成したとされ、仁王門脇の回廊に奪衣婆や獄卒などの像が並ぶと紹介される。公式ページで回廊単体の情報が薄いため、追加時は公開条件・撮影可否の再確認を前提にしたい。
highlights: 約200年前から伝わる木像で地獄を表現する回廊
奪衣婆や獄卒などの像が並ぶ地獄めぐり
厄除観音として知られる古刹の中にある異色の造形
from_kameyama: 車で約260分（東名阪→中央道→長野道）
summary_en: A 'hell-tour' corridor inside the ancient temple of Gofuku-ji in Uchida, Matsumoto. Local reporting describes a corridor — completed in 2022 — that uses wooden images handed down for some 200 years to depict the hells, with figures such as Datsue-ba (the old woman who strips the dead) and the hell wardens lined up along a passage beside the Nio gate. Because the temple's own pages give the corridor only sparse coverage, opening hours and photography conditions should be confirmed again before any visit.
highlights_en: A corridor that depicts the hells using wooden images handed down for about 200 years
Figures of Datsue-ba and hell wardens lined along the passage
A striking devotional installation inside a temple long revered as a yakuyoke Kannon site

name: 川倉賽の河原地蔵尊
name_kana: かわくらさいのかわらじぞうそん
address: 青森県五所川原市金木町川倉七夕野426-1
hours: 9:00〜13:00目安、12〜3月休業。大祭時期は混雑に注意
summary: 芦野公園の北東にある小高い丘に、大小約2,000体の地蔵が祀られる霊場。五所川原市公式では、恐山と同様にイタコの霊媒で知られ、旧暦6月22日から24日の例大祭には口寄せを聞く参詣者で賑わうと説明される。賽の河原信仰、死者供養、イタコ文化を一体で扱える津軽の濃い民俗信仰スポット。
highlights: 大小約2,000体の地蔵が集まる霊場
例大祭でイタコの口寄せが行われる
恐山と並び語られる津軽の二大イタコ聖地
from_kameyama: 車で約900分（東名阪→北陸道→日本海東北道→東北道）
summary_en: A sacred site on a low hill northeast of Ashino Park where roughly 2,000 Jizō statues of every size are enshrined. Goshogawara City explains that, like Mt. Osore, the place is known for mediumship by itako shamans, and that during the festival of the 22nd–24th of the sixth lunar month worshippers fill the site to hear the dead spoken through their voices. A dense Tsugaru-region folk shrine where Sai-no-Kawara belief, memorial rites for the dead, and itako culture all converge.
highlights_en: A sacred ground gathering some 2,000 Jizō statues of all sizes
Itako spirit-summoning performed at the great festival
One of Tsugaru's two great itako sanctuaries, spoken of alongside Mt. Osore

name: 真駒内滝野霊園（頭大仏・モアイ像）
name_kana: まこまないたきのれいえん（あたまだいぶつ・もあいぞう）
address: 北海道札幌市南区滝野2
fee: 園内見学は無料、頭大仏殿は拝観寄付金あり
summary: 公益社団法人が運営する巨大公園霊園に、モアイ像、ストーンヘンジ、六地蔵、観音像、安藤忠雄設計の頭大仏殿が並ぶ。公式によれば1981年開園で東京ドーム約20個分の広大な敷地を持ち、大型のモアイ像は高さ9.5m・重さ120t、頭大仏殿は高さ13.5m・重さ1,500tの大仏の頭だけが丘から突き出す独自形式となっている。霊園でありながら巨大モニュメント群が点在するスケール感が異様。
highlights: 高さ9.5m級のモアイ像が並ぶ正門周辺
安藤忠雄設計の丘に包まれた頭大仏殿
霊園内にストーンヘンジや世界各国のモニュメントが点在
from_kameyama: 車＋フェリー約18時間 / 飛行機＋レンタカー約5時間（中部国際空港→新千歳）
summary_en: A vast public-park cemetery, run by a public-interest body, where moai statues, a stonehenge, six Jizō, Kannon images and the Tadao Andō-designed Atama Daibutsu (Head Buddha) hall stand side by side. Per the official site, the larger moai reach 9.5 m tall and 120 t, and the Head Buddha hall is 13.5 m high and weighs 1,500 t, with only the giant Buddha's head emerging from a hill. A modern memorial space whose scale and mash-up of monuments is genuinely uncanny.
highlights_en: Moai statues up to about 9.5 m tall lining the main gate
The Tadao Andō-designed Atama Daibutsu, its head rising from a hill
Stonehenge and monuments from around the world scattered across the cemetery grounds

name: 蜂天国
name_kana: はちてんごく
address: 長野県東御市加沢435-1
fee: 大人300円、中高生100円、小学生以下無料
summary: 世界初のスズメバチの芸術館を名乗る私設展示施設。公式サイトでは、奇作・傑作600点余の蜂の巣を展示すると説明される。蜂の生態を利用しながら、巨大な巣を造形物として見せる発想が強烈で、自然物・執念・B級展示が重なる。経営母体は隣接の生コン会社（塩沢グループ）で、8〜10月には実際の巣作りの様子も観察できる。
highlights: スズメバチの巣を使った世界初の蜂芸術館
奇作・傑作600点余の蜂の巣展示
8〜10月は実際の巣作りを観察可能
from_kameyama: 車で約300分（東名阪→中央道→長野道→上信越道）
summary_en: A privately run exhibition hall that bills itself as the world's first 'art museum of hornet nests'. The official site states that some 600-plus extraordinary hornet-nest works are on display. The idea of treating giant hornet nests — built by the hornets themselves — as sculptural objects is striking, sitting at the intersection of natural form, sheer obsession, and B-class display. It is run by an affiliated ready-mix concrete company, and from August through October real nest-building can also be observed.
highlights_en: The self-styled world's first art museum of hornet nests
Some 600-plus extraordinary hornet-nest works on display
Real nest-building visible from August through October

name: 西生寺（即身仏と雷獣のミイラ）
name_kana: さいしょうじ（そくしんぶつとらいじゅうのみいら）
address: 新潟県長岡市寺泊野積8996
fee: 大人・高校生500円、小中学生300円
hours: 受付9:00〜15:30、12月中旬〜3月末は冬期休観
summary: 弥彦山中腹の古刹に、弘智法印の即身仏が祀られる。公式サイトでは、弘智法印が3,000日に及ぶ修行ののち貞治2年(1363年)に御入定し、日本最古の即身仏として現在に至ると説明される。宝物堂には雷獣のミイラも伝わり、死と祈り、民間伝承、寺宝展示が濃く重なる。
highlights: 弘智法印の即身仏を祀る越後の古刹
雷獣のミイラを伝える宝物堂
親鸞上人・松尾芭蕉も参詣した日本最古とされる即身仏
from_kameyama: 車で約420分（東名阪→名神→北陸道）
summary_en: An old temple on the flank of Mt. Yahiko enshrining the sokushinbutsu (self-mummified Buddhist monk) of Kōchi Hōin. The official site states that, after 3,000 days of austerities, Kōchi Hōin entered nyūjō in 1363 and has remained as a sokushinbutsu ever since. The treasure hall also preserves a so-called 'raijū' (thunder beast) mummy — death, prayer, folk lore and temple-treasure display layered densely in a single site.
highlights_en: An old Echigo temple enshrining the sokushinbutsu of Kōchi Hōin
A treasure hall preserving a so-called raijū (thunder beast) mummy
What is regarded as Japan's oldest sokushinbutsu, visited historically by Shinran and Bashō

name: 高鍋大師
name_kana: たかなべだいし
address: 宮崎県児湯郡高鍋町大字持田
summary: 持田古墳群の近くに、故・岩岡保吉氏が私財を投じ還暦過ぎから一人で彫り上げた約750体の神仏混合の石像群が並ぶ。みやざき観光ナビは、盗掘被害にあった古墳に眠る古代人の霊を慰めるために1933年から開山されたと説明する。素朴でデフォルメの強い造形と鎮魂の動機が重なる、全国版向けの強いB級・民俗信仰スポット。
highlights: 約750体の神仏混合の石像群
持田古墳群の盗掘被害への鎮魂を背景にした造形
個人の信仰とアウトサイダーアートが重なる丘
from_kameyama: 車で約760分（新名神→山陽道→九州道→東九州道）
summary_en: Near the Mochida burial mound cluster stands a hill covered in some 750 syncretic stone images, carved single-handedly by the late Iwaoka Yasukichi from his sixties onward. Miyazaki Kankō Navi explains that he began the work in 1933 to console the spirits of the ancients buried in the looted tombs. Naïve, strongly deformed forms meet a motive of memorial — a powerfully Bizarre Japan-grade folk and outsider-art site, fit for the national edition.
highlights_en: Around 750 syncretic Buddhist–Shinto stone images
Founded as a memorial to the looting of the Mochida burial mounds
A hillside where private devotion and outsider art converge

name: 福田海 鼻ぐり塚
name_kana: ふくでんかい はなぐりづか
address: 岡山県岡山市北区吉備津795
summary: 宗教法人福田海本部の境内に、全国から集められた牛の鼻輪を積み上げた供養塚。岡山観光WEBとるるぶは、牛の鼻輪約700万個を積み上げ、馬頭観音を本尊に家畜全般を供養していると説明する。色とりどりの鼻ぐりが巨大な塚を成す光景は異様だが、根底には食と畜類への鎮魂がある。
highlights: 約700万個の牛の鼻輪を積み上げた供養塚
馬頭観音を本尊に家畜全般を供養
食肉・畜産と鎮魂が可視化された大量奉納空間
from_kameyama: 車で約220分（新名神→山陽道）
summary_en: Within the grounds of the religious corporation Fukudenkai stands a memorial mound piled from cattle nose rings gathered from all over Japan. Okayama Kankō Web and Rurubu describe it as a heap of roughly seven million cattle nose rings, dedicated to Batō Kannon as the chief deity in memorial for livestock of every kind. The mound — bright with countless coloured rings — appears outlandish, but at its core lies a serious requiem for meat-producing animals.
highlights_en: A memorial mound piled with around seven million cattle nose rings
Dedicated to Batō Kannon as chief deity, memorialising all livestock
A mass-offering space where meat, livestock husbandry, and requiem are made visible

name: 陰陽石
name_kana: いんようせき
address: 宮崎県小林市東方
summary: 小林市の川沿いに、男石・女石と呼ばれる巨大な自然石が並ぶ奇岩信仰の場。小林市公式によれば男石(陽石)は高さ17.5m、女石(陰石)は周囲5.5mで、霧島火山帯の加久藤火砕流が浸食を受けて形成された自然奇岩とされる。生殖器象徴が明瞭で、子宝・縁結び・夫婦和合の祈願対象として古くから語られる。霧島ジオパークのジオサイト指定。
highlights: 男石(高さ17.5m)・女石(周囲5.5m)と呼ばれる巨大自然石
子宝・縁結び・夫婦和合の祈願対象
霧島ジオパークのジオサイト指定
from_kameyama: 車で約780分（新名神→山陽道→九州道→宮崎道）
summary_en: Along a riverbank in Kobayashi City stand a pair of huge natural rocks known as the Male Stone and the Female Stone — a place of cult around an extraordinary natural formation. According to the City of Kobayashi, the male (yang) stone is about 17.5 m tall and the female (yin) stone about 5.5 m around, formed when the Kakutō pyroclastic flow of the Kirishima volcanic belt was eroded by the river. The phallic and yonic forms are unmistakable, and the rocks have long been worshipped for child-blessing, matchmaking and conjugal harmony. They are also designated as a Kirishima Geopark geosite.
highlights_en: Massive natural rocks known as the Male Stone (17.5 m tall) and the Female Stone (5.5 m around)
An object of prayer for children, matchmaking, and harmony between spouses
Designated as a Kirishima Geopark geosite

name: 軽部神社
name_kana: かるべじんじゃ
address: 岡山県総社市清音軽部
summary: 延宝6年(1678年)建立とされる神社で、境内にあった『垂乳根の桜』にちなみ乳神様として信仰を集めた。総社市公式は、安産や母乳の出を願うため、乳房を型取った手作りの絵馬が数多く奉納されていると説明する。身体信仰と大量奉納が視覚的に残る、岡山の民俗信仰スポット。
highlights: 乳神様として信仰される神社
乳房を型取った手作り絵馬が多数奉納
安産・母乳・乳の病に関わる身体信仰
from_kameyama: 車で約210分（新名神→山陽道）
summary_en: A shrine said to have been founded in 1678, which gathered devotion as 'the breast deity' from a 'Tarachine cherry tree' once standing in its precincts. The City of Sōja explains that worshippers praying for safe birth and for breast milk dedicate many hand-made ema in the shape of breasts. A folk-belief site where bodily devotion and mass votive offerings remain vividly visible.
highlights_en: A shrine venerated as 'the breast deity'
Many hand-made ema shaped like breasts dedicated as offerings
Bodily devotion tied to safe birth, breast milk, and breast ailments

name: 淡嶋神社
name_kana: あわしまじんじゃ
address: 和歌山県和歌山市加太118
fee: 無料、宝物殿は300円(要予約)
summary: 和歌山市加太に鎮座し、全国の淡島神社・粟島神社の総本社とされる神社。少彦名命を祭神とし、医薬・安産・女性の病気回復の信仰と結びつく。境内には全国から納められた2万体以上の人形が並び、3月3日には人形を船に乗せ海に流す『雛流し』神事が行われる。心霊的に煽られやすい場所だが、Bizarre Japanでは人形供養と女性信仰の民俗として扱う。
highlights: 境内を埋める2万体以上の奉納人形
全国淡島神社・粟島神社の総本社
3月3日『雛流し』神事と2月8日『針供養』
from_kameyama: 車で約150分（名阪国道→京奈和道→阪和道）
summary_en: A shrine in Kada, Wakayama City, known nationwide for memorial rites for dolls. Within its precincts stand great numbers of dolls offered from all over Japan, in connection with prayers for recovery from women's illnesses and for safe birth. Although the site is easily framed in 'haunted' terms, on Bizarre Japan we treat it strictly as a folk-religion site of doll memorial and women's devotion.
highlights_en: Precincts filled with great numbers of offered dolls
A Kada shrine famed for ningyō-kuyō (doll memorial)
Tied to belief in recovery from women's illnesses and safe birth

name: 飯野高宮神山神社
name_kana: いいのたかみやこうやまじんじゃ
address: 三重県松阪市山添町4
summary: 松阪市山添町に鎮座する式内社とされる神社。境内・参道をJR紀勢本線が物理的に横断する構造が特異で、神域と近代鉄道が同じ空間で交差する珍しい事例として知られる。線路横断部は踏切扱いで、参拝の際は安全に配慮する必要がある。神社そのものの祭祀は通常の式内社として継承されている。
highlights: 境内・参道をJR紀勢本線が横切る稀な構造
式内社とされる古社で神域に鉄道が交差
踏切を渡って参拝する形になる動線
from_kameyama: 車で約1時間（伊勢道→国道166号方面）
summary_en: A shrine in Yamazoe-cho, Matsusaka, said to be a shikinaisha (listed in the 10th-century Engishiki). Its distinguishing feature is that the JR Kisei Main Line physically crosses the shrine precincts and approach path, creating an unusual case in which a sacred site and a modern railway share the same ground. Visitors cross at a level crossing on the way to worship; ritual life at the shrine itself continues in the ordinary manner of a shikinaisha.
highlights_en: A rare layout in which the JR Kisei Main Line crosses through the precincts and approach
A shikinaisha-class old shrine whose sacred area intersects an active rail line
Worshippers must cross a railway level crossing to reach the shrine

name: 珍布峠と礫石
name_kana: めずらしとうげ と つぶていし
address: 三重県松阪市飯高町赤桶
hours: 通行自由（旧街道ウォーキング推奨時間帯）
summary: 松阪市飯高町赤桶にある旧街道の切り通し峠と、それに付随する伝承石「礫石」。観光三重は、天照大神と天児屋根命がこの地で出会い「おお、めずらしや」と言ったことから珍布峠と呼ばれると説明し、さらに天照大神が川に投げ入れて国境を決めたと伝わる礫石が見える地点として紹介している。地名民俗と国分け伝説が一体で残る場所。
highlights: 「めずらし峠」という地名そのものに残る神話的由来
天照大神と天児屋根命の邂逅伝説
国分け伝説に登場する礫石が川中に残る
from_kameyama: 車で約1時間20分（伊勢道→国道166号方面）
summary_en: An old-road cut-through pass in Akaoke, Iitaka-cho, Matsusaka, paired with the legendary boulder known as Tsubute-ishi. Kanko Mie (the prefectural tourism site) explains that the name 'Mezurashi-toge' comes from a tradition that the deities Amaterasu and Ame-no-Koyane met here and said 'oh, how rare,' and that Tsubute-ishi is the stone Amaterasu is said to have thrown into the river to mark a provincial boundary. The site preserves a place-name folklore and a 'land-dividing' legend together at one location.
highlights_en: A mythic origin embedded in the place-name itself ('Mezurashi Pass')
Tradition of the encounter between Amaterasu and Ame-no-Koyane
Tsubute-ishi, the boulder of the land-dividing legend, visible in the river

name: 鳥羽市民の森公園 ガリバー像
name_kana: とばしみんのもりこうえん がりばーぞう
address: 三重県鳥羽市大明東町
summary: 鳥羽市民の森公園内にある、高さ6m・幅12mの巨大ガリバー遊具。伊勢志摩観光ナビは、全国でも屈指の巨大遊具として、1982年に「鳥羽ぶらじる丸」のガリバー広場でデビューし、1985年に現在の鳥羽市民の森公園へ移されたと説明している。1980年代の海洋パビリオン文化を背景に持つ、公共公園内のB級モニュメント。
highlights: 高さ6m・幅12mの巨大ガリバー造形遊具
1982年「鳥羽ぶらじる丸」由来で1985年に移設された経緯
無料の公共公園内にあるB級モニュメント
from_kameyama: 車で約1時間20分（伊勢道→鳥羽方面）
name_en: Gulliver Figure at Toba Citizen Forest Park
summary_en: A reclining Gulliver play sculpture, 6 m tall and 12 m wide, set inside Toba Citizen Forest Park. Local tourism material describes it as one of the largest figure-form playground installations in Japan, originally unveiled in 1982 at the 'Toba Brazil-Maru' Gulliver Plaza and then moved to its present home at Toba Citizen Forest Park in 1985. It functions as a public-park B-class monument carrying the legacy of 1980s seaside pavilion culture.
highlights_en: An oversized Gulliver figure 6 m tall and 12 m wide
A 1982 origin at the 'Toba Brazil-Maru' Gulliver Plaza, relocated to the park in 1985
A free public park hosting a distinctly B-class monument

name: 阿曽の風穴
name_kana: あそのふうけつ
address: 三重県度会郡大紀町阿曽
summary: 大紀町阿曽にある石灰岩の天然洞窟。観光三重は、長年の水の浸食により石灰岩が作った天然の洞窟で、内部に照明があり気軽に自然の神秘に触れられると説明している。山中の低温空間と石灰岩の造形が見られる小規模な自然奇景で、JR阿曽駅から徒歩約30分の位置にある。
highlights: 水の浸食でできた石灰岩の天然洞窟
内部に照明があり訪問しやすい構造
JR阿曽駅徒歩約30分の山間アクセス
from_kameyama: 車で約1時間30分（伊勢道→紀勢道→大内山IC方面）
summary_en: A natural limestone cave in Aso, Taiki Town. Kanko Mie describes it as a cave formed over a long period by water eroding limestone, fitted with interior lighting that lets visitors encounter the geological feature easily. It is a small-scale natural curiosity—a cool, dim space of water-cut limestone—about a 30-minute walk from JR Aso Station.
highlights_en: A natural limestone cave shaped by long-term water erosion
Interior lighting that makes the cave straightforward to visit
A roughly 30-minute walk from JR Aso Station into the hills

name: 鯨の供養塔
name_kana: くじらのくようとう
address: 三重県熊野市二木島町字相川
fee: 無料（個人所有の文化財・要事前確認）
hours: 要事前確認（熊野市教育委員会）
summary: 熊野市二木島町に立つ江戸期の供養塔。観光三重は、寛文11年（1671年）に作られ正面に「鯨三十三本供養塔」の切付けがあると説明している。熊野の漁撈民俗ではブリ・マグロなど33本を得るごとに「万の祝」と称して祝う風習があり、この供養塔もその記念として建てられたと伝わる。動物供養と海の生業信仰が交差する民俗碑。
highlights: 寛文11年（1671年）銘の「鯨三十三本供養塔」
ブリ・マグロ33本ごとの「万の祝」の習俗
熊野の漁撈民俗と動物供養が一体で残る石碑
from_kameyama: 車で約1時間40分（紀勢道経由）
name_en: Kujira no Kuyo-to Whale Memorial Tower
summary_en: An Edo-period memorial stone in Nigishima-cho, Kumano. Kanko Mie records that it was erected in Kanbun 11 (1671) and that its face is inscribed 'Memorial Tower for Thirty-three Whales.' In Kumano fishing folklore, taking thirty-three of a major fish (yellowtail, tuna, etc.) was celebrated as 'Man-no-Iwai' (Celebration of Ten Thousand), and this tower is said to have been raised in connection with that practice. The site sits at the intersection of animal memorial tradition and the religious life of a fishing community.
highlights_en: An Edo-period stele inscribed 'Memorial Tower for Thirty-three Whales' (1671)
Tied to the 'Man-no-Iwai' custom of marking every 33 yellowtail or tuna caught
A folk-religion stone where animal memorial and maritime livelihood meet

name: 九鬼嘉隆の首塚・胴塚
name_kana: くきよしたかのくびづか・どうづか
address: 三重県鳥羽市答志町（答志島・首塚は築上山山頂、胴塚は和具漁港集落内）
hours: 見学可（島内集落・墓所のため静粛に）
summary: 戦国期の九鬼水軍当主・九鬼嘉隆の首と胴を別々に葬った供養地。観光三重は、首が遺言どおり鳥羽城を一望できる築上山山頂に埋葬されたと説明し、伊勢志摩観光ナビは、胴を葬った墓が和具漁港の町中にあり、洞仙庵跡や血洗い池が近くにあると説明している。首塚と胴塚が島内で離れている点が特異で、身体分離の葬送と島の記憶を伝える民俗的史跡として読める。
highlights: 首塚は築上山山頂、胴塚は和具漁港集落内に分かれて所在
首塚は鳥羽城を一望できる位置に置かれた遺言伝承
胴塚周辺に洞仙庵跡と血洗い池が残る
from_kameyama: 車＋市営定期船＋徒歩で約2時間（亀山→鳥羽マリンターミナル→答志島）
name_en: Kuki Yoshitaka Head Mound and Body Mound
summary_en: Two separate burial sites on Tōshi-jima island, where the head and body of the Sengoku-era Kuki navy commander Kuki Yoshitaka were interred apart. Kanko Mie records that, per his will, his head was buried at the summit of Mt. Tsukijo with a view over Toba Castle; Ise-Shima Kanko Navi explains that the body mound stands in the town center of Wagu Fishing Port, near the former Tōsen-an temple site and the 'Chiarai-ike' (blood-washing pond). The fact that the head and body mounds are physically separated on the island makes this site readable as a folk-religion record of a divided-body burial tradition, rather than as an ordinary samurai gravesite.
highlights_en: Head Mound at the summit of Mt. Tsukijo; Body Mound in Wagu Port village
Head burial chosen to overlook Toba Castle, per Kuki Yoshitaka's will
Former Tōsen-an temple site and 'Chiarai-ike' pond remain near the body mound

name: 御塩殿神社・御塩道
name_kana: みしおどのじんじゃ・みしおみち
address: 三重県伊勢市二見町荘
fee: 無料（参拝可能範囲は要確認）
hours: 境内自由（御塩焼所・御塩殿の見学可否は要確認）
summary: 伊勢神宮内宮の所管社で、神宮の祭典で使用する堅塩を製造する場所。伊勢市観光協会は、高濃度の塩水を御塩焼所で煮つめて荒塩にし、御塩殿で型に入れて焼き固めると説明している。御塩浜で採られた塩水が御塩道を通って御塩殿まで運ばれる流れまで含めると、神宮周縁の特殊な生産儀礼として民俗信仰の核心が見える場所。
highlights: 神宮の祭典で使う堅塩を製造する内宮所管社
御塩焼所での荒塩煮詰めと御塩殿での焼き固めの二段工程
御塩浜から御塩殿へ続く「御塩道」の存在
from_kameyama: 車で約60分（伊勢道→伊勢二見鳥羽ライン→二見方面）
name_en: Mishiodono Shrine and Sacred Salt Route
summary_en: An auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine (Naikū) of Ise Jingū, devoted to the production of the hardened salt (katashio) used in Jingū rituals. The Ise City Tourist Association describes a two-stage process: highly concentrated brine is reduced to coarse salt at the Mishio-yakisho, then molded and fire-hardened at the Mishio-dono itself. Read together with the Mishio-michi (Sacred Salt Route) along which brine from the Mishio-hama salt fields is carried to the shrine, the site forms an unusual production-ritual complex on the periphery of Jingū.
highlights_en: An auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine where ritual hardened salt is produced
A two-stage process: reduction at the Mishio-yakisho and firing at the Mishio-dono
The 'Mishio-michi' route along which brine is carried from the salt fields

name: 陶芸空間 虹の泉
name_kana: とうげいくうかん にじのいずみ
address: 三重県松阪市飯高町波瀬（国道166号沿い）
fee: 高校生以上800円（2025年4月改定、それ以前は500円）
summary: 陶芸家・東健次が松阪市飯高町波瀬の山中に約35年をかけて築いた屋外陶芸空間。観光三重は、5,800㎡規模の土地を陶で埋め尽くすことを目指したが2013年に作家が急逝し未完の大作となったと説明している。2023年4月から保存維持活動が再開され、2025年4月から入館料が改定された。受付不在時は近隣の道の駅でチケットを購入する運用が紹介されており、未完のまま屋外に残る陶芸環境という点で他の美術館とは性格が異なる。
highlights: 陶芸家・東健次が35年かけて築いた未完の屋外陶芸空間
5,800㎡の敷地を陶で埋め尽くす構想と作家急逝による未完性
2023年から保存維持活動が再開、2025年4月から入館料改定
from_kameyama: 車で約2時間（名阪国道→伊勢自動車道松阪IC→国道166号方面）
name_en: Niji no Izumi Ceramic Art Space
summary_en: An outdoor ceramic art space built by potter Kenji Higashi over roughly 35 years in the mountains of Hase, Iitaka-cho, Matsusaka City. Kanko Mie records that Higashi aimed to cover a 5,800-square-meter site entirely in ceramic works, but his sudden death in 2013 left it unfinished. Preservation activities resumed in April 2023, and the admission fee was revised in April 2025. When the reception is unstaffed, tickets can be purchased at the nearby roadside station. Unlike a conventional museum, the site is best understood as an unfinished outdoor ceramic environment.
highlights_en: An unfinished outdoor ceramic environment built over 35 years by potter Kenji Higashi
Plan to cover the 5,800 m² site entirely in ceramics, left incomplete after the artist's death
Preservation activity resumed in 2023; admission fees revised in April 2025

name: 丹倉神社
name_kana: あかぐらじんじゃ
address: 三重県熊野市育生町赤倉
hours: 境内自由（駐車場・トイレなし、山中アクセスのため要注意）
summary: 熊野市育生町赤倉、赤倉集落のさらに山深い奥に鎮座する古社。熊野市観光公社は、熊野三山に参拝者が訪れる以前からこの地に根付いた磐座信仰を示す巨岩をご神体とし、岩の上から樹木が育つ生命力も紹介していると説明している。社殿中心ではなく巨岩そのものが信仰対象である点で、熊野地域の自然崇拝と原始的な磐座信仰を現場で体感できる小規模な聖地。駐車場・トイレともになく、訪問は車利用と山中の狭路に注意が必要。
highlights: 巨岩をご神体とする磐座信仰の神社
熊野三山以前から続くとされる古い自然崇拝の場
巨岩の上から樹木が育つ独特の景観
from_kameyama: 車で約3時間（伊勢自動車道→紀勢自動車道→熊野尾鷲道路熊野大泊IC→県道40号→育生町赤倉方面）
summary_en: A small, ancient shrine deep in the mountains beyond the Akakura settlement in Ikuo-cho, Kumano City. The Kumano City Tourism Association explains that the shrine takes a massive boulder as its sacred body, reflecting an iwakura (sacred-rock) tradition rooted in the area before pilgrims began visiting the Kumano Sanzan, and notes the striking sight of trees growing from the top of the rock. Worship here centers on the boulder itself rather than a main hall, making the site readable as a small but vivid example of Kumano's archaic nature worship. There is no parking and no toilet; access requires a car and care on narrow mountain roads.
highlights_en: A shrine where a massive boulder serves as the sacred body of the kami (iwakura worship)
An ancient nature-worship site said to predate the Kumano Sanzan pilgrimage tradition
Distinctive landscape of trees growing on top of the sacred boulder

name: 徐福ノ宮（波田須）
name_kana: じょふくのみや（はだす）
address: 三重県熊野市波田須町（蓬莱山）
hours: 境内自由（駐車場3台・トイレなし）
summary: 熊野市波田須町の蓬莱山（丸山）に鎮座し、秦の始皇帝の命で不老不死の霊薬を求めて渡来したと伝えられる徐福を祀る小祠。熊野市公式は徐福ノ宮を歴史を体感する観光資源として紹介し、熊野市観光公社は波田須町に駐車場3台・トイレなしで、JR熊野市駅から車約20分、JR波田須駅から徒歩約10分と案内している。徐福伝来は伝承であり史実として確定しているわけではないが、中国大陸からの渡来人を神として祀る構図と、熊野古道伊勢路・波田須の集落史が重なる小スポット。
highlights: 渡来伝承の徐福を祀る波田須の小祠
蓬莱山（丸山）に位置し、熊野古道伊勢路・波田須宿に近い
駐車場3台のみ、トイレなしの小規模信仰地
from_kameyama: 車で約2時間40分（伊勢自動車道→紀勢自動車道→熊野尾鷲道路→国道311号波田須方面）
summary_en: A small shrine on Mt. Hōrai (also called Maruyama) in Hadasu-cho, Kumano City, dedicated to Xu Fu (Jofuku), a figure said to have been sent by China's Qin Shi Huang in search of an elixir of immortality and to have settled in this area. The City of Kumano introduces Jofuku-no-Miya as a heritage tourism resource, and the Kumano City Tourism Association notes that the site has parking for three cars, no toilet, and is reached in about 20 minutes by car from JR Kumano Station or roughly a 10-minute walk from JR Hadasu Station. The Xu Fu story is a local tradition rather than confirmed history, but the shrine is notable as a small site that enshrines a continental immigrant as a deity, set within the village landscape of the Iseji route of the Kumano Kodō.
highlights_en: A small shrine in Hadasu enshrining the legendary continental immigrant Xu Fu (Jofuku)
Located on Mt. Hōrai (Maruyama), near the Iseji route of the Kumano Kodō and the Hadasu post-village
Modest devotional site with only three parking spaces and no toilet

name: 朝田寺 掛衣奉納群
name_kana: ちょうでんじ かけころもほうのうぐん
address: 三重県松阪市朝田町427
fee: 無料・境内自由（道明供養は11月24日）
hours: 境内終日自由。掛衣群は通年見学可。お焚き上げ「道明供養」は毎年11月24日
summary: 松阪市朝田町に所在する天台真盛宗の古刹。本尊地蔵菩薩は重要文化財。境内・本堂内に故人の遺愛の衣類を掛けて死後の道明け（成仏）を祈る「掛衣（かけころも）」の風習で知られ、本堂・庫裏・境内の木々に大量の着物・洋服・帯が吊り下げられた光景が異様。掛けられた衣類は毎年11月24日の「道明供養（どうみょうくよう）」で焚き上げられる。県指定文化財の本堂（慶安5年/1652建立、入母屋造本瓦葺）、曽我蕭白の襖絵、花の寺としても知られるが、本Spaceでは衣類奉納の民俗信仰の物証性に注目する。怪奇煽りではなく死者鎮魂の文脈で扱うべき例。
highlights: 境内の樹木・堂内に故人の衣類が大量に掛けられた異様な光景
11月24日の「道明供養」で奉納衣類をお焚き上げ
三重県指定文化財・慶安5年（1652）建立の本堂、曽我蕭白襖絵
from_kameyama: 車で約75分（伊勢自動車道→松阪IC→国道166号、松阪市朝田町）
summary_en: An ancient Tendai-Shinsei sect temple in Asada-cho, Matsusaka. The principal Jizo Bosatsu statue is a National Important Cultural Property. The temple is known for the 'kake-koromo' (hanging garment) tradition: bereaved families hang the beloved clothes of the deceased in the main hall, on the storehouse, and from the trees in the grounds, praying for the dead to find their way to enlightenment. The result is a striking sight of countless kimono, Western clothes, and obi sashes hanging throughout the precincts. Offered garments are burned each year on November 24 in the 'Domyo Kuyo' memorial rite. The main hall (built 1652, hip-and-gable roof with tile) is a Mie Prefectural Cultural Property, and fusuma paintings by Soga Shohaku are kept here. This entry treats the site through the folkloric lens of memorialization of the dead — not as the supernatural.
highlights_en: Trees and halls hung with great numbers of the deceased's garments — a striking sight
Offerings are burned on November 24 each year in the 'Domyo Kuyo' memorial ceremony
Mie Prefectural Cultural Property main hall (built 1652); fusuma paintings by Soga Shohaku

name: 町屋百人衆 巨大龍踊り（千王神社）
name_kana: まちやひゃくにんしゅう きょだいりゅうおどり（せんのうじんじゃ）
address: 三重県津市栗真町屋町946（千王神社）
status: 現存（毎年7月開催／存続課題あり）
hours: 毎年7月の祇園祭（土曜夜）
summary: 津市栗真町屋町の千王神社（せんのうじんじゃ）の祇園祭で奉納される全長約55mの電飾巨大龍。地元有志「町屋百人衆」が担ぎ手となり、伊勢街道から国道23号にかけて夜の街道を練り歩く。雨乞いと地域結束の意義を担う近現代の民俗行事で、保存会の高齢化と担い手不足により存続が課題となっており、クラウドファンディングでの支援活動も行われている。本Spaceでは「55mの龍が市街地国道を練り歩く視覚インパクト」と「地元100人で支える担い手の物語」の双方の文脈で扱う。
highlights: 全長約55mの電飾龍が国道23号を練り歩く
千王神社祇園祭（毎年7月）で奉納される地域行事
地元有志「町屋百人衆」が担ぎ手・担い手不足で存続課題
from_kameyama: 車で約50分（伊勢自動車道→津IC→国道23号北上、津市栗真町屋町）
name_en: Machiya Hyakuninshu — Giant Dragon Dance (Seno Shrine)
summary_en: A roughly 55-meter-long electric-lit giant dragon dance offered at the Gion festival of Seno Shrine in Kurima-Machiyacho, Tsu City. Local volunteers known as the 'Machiya Hyakuninshu' (literally 'Hundred Townspeople') carry the dragon along the old Ise highway and out to National Route 23 in the evening. A modern folk event that doubles as a rain-prayer and community-bonding rite, it now faces existential challenges as the preservation group ages and successors become scarce; crowdfunding campaigns have been launched to sustain it. This entry frames the dragon as both a powerful nighttime spectacle and a story of community succession.
highlights_en: A 55-meter electric dragon paraded down National Route 23
Offered at Seno Shrine's Gion Festival every July
Carried by local volunteers known as the 'Machiya Hyakuninshu' — at risk due to aging and lack of successors

name: 海山道稲荷神社（千体狐とコンビナート）
name_kana: みやまどいなりじんじゃ（せんたいきつねとこんびなーと）
address: 三重県四日市市海山道町1-62（海山道神社内・海山道開運稲荷神社）
hours: 境内終日自由（社務所は日中）
summary: 四日市市海山道町に鎮座する神社。境内社の「海山道開運稲荷神社」のほうが本社より大きく、全国から奉納された無数の狐像が境内を埋め尽くす視覚密度が異様。背後には四日市コンビナートの煙突群が広がり、稲荷信仰と戦後重化学工業景観が一枚の画角に収まる三重独自の「異界」風景を形成する。近鉄名古屋線「海山道」駅前で撮影アクセス良好。通称「みやまどさん」。
highlights: 境内を埋め尽くす千体規模の奉納狐像
背景に四日市コンビナート煙突群が広がる異色の構図
近鉄海山道駅前で通年撮影可能・通称「みやまどさん」
from_kameyama: 車で約40分（東名阪自動車道→四日市東IC→国道23号南下、四日市市海山道町）
name_en: Miyamado Inari Shrine (Thousand Foxes & Petrochemical Skyline)
summary_en: A shrine in Miyamadocho, Yokkaichi City. The auxiliary 'Miyamado Kaiun Inari Shrine' on the grounds is actually larger than the main hall; its precincts are packed with hundreds of fox statues donated from across Japan, producing an overwhelming visual density. Behind the shrine rise the chimneys of the Yokkaichi petrochemical complex, and in a single frame, an old fox-cult shrine and postwar heavy industry coexist — a peculiarly Mie 'otherworldly' landscape. Excellent photography access right in front of Kintetsu Miyamado Station. Locally nicknamed 'Miyamado-san.'
highlights_en: Roughly a thousand donated fox statues packed across the grounds
Yokkaichi petrochemical chimneys form a striking backdrop in a single frame
Open year-round, in front of Kintetsu Miyamado Station — locally 'Miyamado-san'

name: 仙宮神社・猿神石
name_kana: せんぐうじんじゃ・さるしんせき
address: 三重県度会郡南伊勢町河内77
hours: 境内終日自由（社務所は日中）
summary: 度会郡南伊勢町河内に鎮座する古社。標高約70mの山の上にあり、御神木「蘇りの木」と呼ばれるバクチノキの横から本殿へと360段余りの石段が続く。本殿背後の磐山には、猿田彦命が岩となってこの地を守るという伝承を持つ巨岩「猿神石（さるしんせき）」が露出し、猿の横顔に見える形状を示す。本殿背後の磐座群は古代の祭祀跡で多くの遺物が出土しており、文化財の神社遺跡に指定。絵馬殿には百人一首や天の岩戸の神話にちなんだ絵画が奉納されている。
highlights: 猿田彦命が岩となったとされる「猿神石」（巨岩・磐座信仰）
本殿背後の磐山は古代祭祀跡・出土遺物多数・文化財指定
御神木「蘇りの木」（バクチノキ）と360段余の石段
from_kameyama: 車で約130分（伊勢自動車道→玉城IC→国道260号、南伊勢町河内）
summary_en: An ancient shrine in Kawachi, Minami-Ise Town in Watarai District. Perched atop a roughly 70-meter hill, the main hall is reached by a flight of over 360 stone steps that begin beside the sacred 'Tree of Resurrection' (a Bakuchi tree). On the rocky slope behind the main hall, a massive rock outcrop known as 'Sarushinseki' (Monkey God Stone) bears a profile resembling a monkey's face, said in local tradition to be the deity Sarutahiko transformed into stone to guard the land. The iwakura (sacred rock) group behind the main hall is an ancient ritual site that has yielded many artifacts and is designated a cultural property as a shrine archaeological site. The picture-tablet hall displays paintings inspired by the Hyakunin Isshu poems and the Tale of the Heavenly Rock Cave.
highlights_en: 'Sarushinseki' (Monkey God Stone): a massive iwakura associated with Sarutahiko
Iwakura group behind the main hall — an ancient ritual site with abundant excavated artifacts, designated as a cultural property
Sacred 'Tree of Resurrection' (Bakuchi tree) and the 360-plus stone steps

name: 中尾歯科（四日市市・異形歯科外観）
name_kana: なかおしか（よっかいちし・いぎょうしかがいかん）
address: 三重県四日市市松本3丁目9-9（中尾歯科 まつもと診療所を代表座標として登録／複数店舗あり）
hours: 店舗ごとに異なる（夜10時まで営業の店舗あり）
summary: 医療法人良友会が運営する四日市市・鈴鹿市の歯科チェーン。複数店舗にわたり、外壁の巨大な歯のオブジェ、煌びやかな内外装、夜10時までの営業など、一般的な医療施設の枠を大きく外れた視覚的存在感で知られる。店舗ごとに看板の意匠が異なり、四日市市内では複数の路面店が「異形の歯科」として地元のランドマーク化している。本エントリは代表座標として「まつもと診療所」を採用し、本文では他店舗の看板・外観にも言及する統合掲載方針。座標は松本3丁目中心の代表値。
highlights: 外壁の巨大な歯のオブジェと派手な内外装
夜10時まで営業の店舗もあり通常の歯科の枠外
四日市市・鈴鹿市に複数店舗を展開する歯科チェーン
from_kameyama: 車で約40分（東名阪自動車道→四日市IC→国道477号、四日市市松本方面）
name_en: Nakao Dental Clinic (Yokkaichi — Surreal Clinic Facades)
summary_en: A dental clinic chain operated by the medical corporation Ryoyu-kai across Yokkaichi and Suzuka cities, known for a visual presence that breaks far outside the look of a typical medical facility: giant tooth sculptures on the exterior, glittering interiors and signage, and branches that stay open as late as 10 PM. Each branch sports a distinct signboard design, and several street-level branches in Yokkaichi function as local landmarks of 'unusual dental clinic' aesthetics. This entry uses the Matsumoto branch as a representative location and refers to other branch facades in the description. Coordinates are a representative value for the center of Matsumoto 3-chome.
highlights_en: Giant tooth sculptures on the exterior and flamboyant interior decor
Some branches stay open until 10 PM — well outside ordinary dental clinic norms
Multiple branches across Yokkaichi and Suzuka, each with distinct signage

name: 十宝山大乗院（鬼のミイラ）
name_kana: じっぽうざんだいじょういん（おにのみいら）
address: 〒872-0014 大分県宇佐市大字四日市3761
summary: 大分県宇佐市四日市にある十宝山大乗院は、境内の厨子に安置された「鬼のミイラ」で知られる真言宗系の寺院。ミイラは座高約1.4m（立てば2mを超えると推定）、手足の指はいずれも3本、額に長さ5cmの角が生え、目が落ち窪み、口が半開きになった独特の形状を持つ。「鬼さん」と地元で親しまれ、信仰対象として安置されている。大正14年（1925年）に檀家が下関市での売買証書に品名「鬼形骨」と記された状態で5500円で購入し、昭和4〜5年頃に原因不明の病に罹ったことを契機に寺に寄贈したと伝わる。昭和初期に九州大学で鑑定が行われ、「女性の人骨と動物の骨で構成されているのでは」との見解が示されたが、現在も正体は特定されていない。寺へは境内入口から108段の急石段を上る。撮影は禁止。宇佐ICから車で約5分。
highlights: 座高1.4m・3本指・5cmの角を持つ正体不明の「鬼のミイラ」が厨子に安置
大正時代に5500円で購入され、昭和初期に九州大学で鑑定された経緯を持つ
108段の急石段を上った先にある小さな寺に、地元で「鬼さん」と呼ばれ祀られている
from_kameyama: 車で約4時間（伊勢自動車道→東名阪→名阪国道→西名阪→阪神高速→大分道→宇佐IC、約350km）。または新幹線と特急を乗り継いで宇佐駅下車後、タクシー約10分。

deepdive.history_jp: 十宝山大乗院は宇佐市四日市に所在する小寺で、創建年代は不詳。伝承によれば、安置される「鬼のミイラ」は元々ある家の所有物であり、大正14年（1925年）に大乗院の檀家が下関市での売買を示す証書（品名「鬼形骨」）に基づき5500円で購入したとされる（[日本伝承大鑑・japanmystery.com](https://japanmystery.com/ooita/mummy.html)）。購入直後から檀家が原因不明の病に倒れ、昭和4〜5年頃にミイラを寺へ寄贈した途端に回復したとも語られる。住職は「子供の頃に山で出会った鬼が帰ってきた」と話したという（同前）。昭和初期、九州大学による鑑定では「女性の人骨と動物の骨で構成されているのでは」との見解が示されたが、正体は確定していない。現在は信仰の対象として本堂に安置されており、寺院の公式的な宝物として展示されているのではなく、あくまでも「持仏」として扱われている（[宇佐市公式](https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10170.html)）。テレビで複数回放映されたことで全国的に知られるようになり、休日には遠方からの参拝者も訪れる（同前）。
deepdive.cultural_context_jp: 「鬼形骨」という売買証書上の名称が示すように、このミイラは近代以前から「鬼」として流通・売買された民俗的な「珍物」の一例と位置付けられる。江戸後期から明治期にかけて、人魚・河童・鬼の類のミイラが各地で寺社に寄贈・展示された例は複数知られており、多くは剥製技術を応用して複数の動物骨・皮・毛を組み合わせて造型されたものとされる（[japanmystery.com](https://japanmystery.com/ooita/mummy.html)）。しかし大乗院のミイラは「寺宝としての展示」ではなく、住職や地域住民が「鬼さん」と呼んで信仰対象とし続けている点において、民俗信仰の文脈で捉えるべき存在である。鬼を「異界から来た使者・守護者」として祀る信仰形態は、東北・九州各地に事例がある。
deepdive.local_perspective_jp: 宇佐市観光・ブランド課が公式観光情報として紹介しており（[宇佐市公式](https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10170.html)）、市の観光スポット一覧に掲載されている。大分県観光情報公式サイト「visit-oita.jp」にも掲載があり（[visit-oita.jp](https://www.visit-oita.jp/spots/detail/5452)）、地域の観光資源として位置付けられている。ただし寺は無住または管理者が対応しており、拝観時間内は本堂扉が開いていて自由に参拝できる形態をとっている（[Omairi](https://omairi.club/spots/93134)）。「鬼さん」という呼称が示すように、地域社会にとっては怪奇な存在ではなく、土地に根差した信仰対象として受け止められている。

- 全国ロケーションデータベース収録（[NFAJ](https://jl-db.nfaj.go.jp/location/440300072/)）
- テレビ番組複数回放映（番組名・放映年は未確認）
- 『日本の珍スポット大全』（各社ガイド本への掲載多数）

[大分県観光情報サイト・visit-oita](https://www.visit-oita.jp/spots/detail/5452)

deepdive.best_visit_time: 年中無休で9:00〜16:00。混雑は少なく、平日・休日問わず訪問できる。夏の午前中が石段登りには快適。
deepdive.photo_tips: 境内での撮影は可能だが、ミイラ本体の撮影は禁止（[MapFan情報](https://mapfan.com/spots/SCWWW,J,Y0)）。108段の石段と本堂外観は撮影可能。石段下から見上げる構図が参道の雰囲気を伝えやすい。
deepdive.trivia:
- 売買証書上の品名「鬼形骨」という名称が残っており、近代の珍物売買の一例として史料的価値がある。
- 九州大学鑑定の詳細記録は現在のところ公開されていない。
- 宇佐神宮（八幡総本社）から車で数分の距離にあるが、宇佐神宮とは無関係の小寺。
- 参拝時は石段外のお地蔵さん・本堂のご本尊の順に参拝することが推奨されているという（[フォートラベル](https://4travel.jp/dm_shisetsu/11322789)）。
deepdive.warnings_extra: ミイラ本体の写真撮影は禁止。境内は急石段（108段）があり、足腰に不安のある方は注意。無住寺院のため受付対応は最小限。駐車場は10〜15台程度（無料）。
